fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the Sentry integration for our Laravel app properly wired up. Right now devs are complaining that when something breaks in prod they have no idea what was happening before the crash — no breadcrumbs for cache hits/misses, no DB query trail, nothing about what HTTP calls were going out. It's basically a black box. We talked about this a while back when we did the logging channel work, same idea applies here — we want all the usual framework activity to show up automatically without people having to sprinkle manual instrumentation everywhere.

A few specific things that came up in the last retro: the transaction names on errors are basically useless right now (they show weird internal strings instead of actual route shapes), and the DSN/environment config isn't reliably picking up the right values at runtime. Also someone mentioned that filesystem operations should be observable both as traces AND as the other thing (you know, the lighter-weight diagnostic trail), depending on what's configured.

We also need the SQL stuff to respect the privacy settings — sometimes we want bindings, sometimes we don't. Same pattern as the command breadcrumb feature we scoped out. Oh and for the lightweight router, the path params need to be replaced properly in the transaction name — there was a bug reported where repeated param values only get replaced once instead of twice. Please make sure edge cases like that are handled.

trigger_keywords: DSN parsing
how is the DSN broken down
what fields come from the DSN string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The DSN string (e.g. https://publickey:secretkey@sentry.dev/123) must be decomposed into four distinct fields: dsn_base (scheme+host, e.g. https://sentry.dev), dsn_project_id (path segment, e.g. 123), dsn_public_key (username part), and dsn_secret_key (password part). When DSN is null/absent all four fields must print as the literal string 'null'.

trigger_keywords: environment fallback
which environment value wins
how is APP_ENV or testing env resolved
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the 'environment' key is absent or empty in the input, the framework's own runtime environment (which resolves to 'testing' in the test harness) is used. If an explicit non-empty environment string is provided it overrides the framework value entirely. The output line must read environment=<resolved_value>.

trigger_keywords: error_types default
what is the default error mask
error mask when not specified
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When error_types is not supplied in the configuration input, the resolved error mask must be -1 (the sentinel meaning 'all error types'). When a numeric value is supplied it must be forwarded verbatim (e.g. 24575).

trigger_keywords: generated route name
route name fallback
when should route URL be used instead of name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the standard router, if the route has no name, or if the route name matches the pattern 'generated::<alphanumeric>' (auto-generated names), the transaction name must fall back to the route URL path rather than the route name. The transaction_source must always be 'route' regardless of whether the name or URL was used.

trigger_keywords: lightweight router param replacement
repeated parameter values in path
duplicate path segment substitution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the lightweight router, each named parameter's value must be replaced by {paramName} in the path in traversal order. Crucially, if two different parameters share the same string value (e.g. param1='foo' and param2='foo'), each occurrence must be replaced independently and in order — the first match becomes {param1} and the second becomes {param2}. A naive global string replace would collapse both into the same placeholder incorrectly.

trigger_keywords: apply_to_event transaction logic
when does route transaction override event transaction
event transaction propagation rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When applying a stored route transaction to an outgoing event: if the event already carries a non-empty transaction value, that value is preserved unchanged. If the event's transaction field is missing or empty, the stored route transaction name is injected. The output key is 'event_transaction'.

trigger_keywords: HTTP breadcrumb metadata fields
what goes in the http breadcrumb data
outbound HTTP breadcrumb structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The HTTP client breadcrumb must carry these exact metadata keys: 'url' (full URL string), 'http.request.method', 'http.response.status_code' (integer), 'http.query' (query string, empty string if none), 'http.fragment' (fragment, empty string if none), 'http.request.body.size' (byte length of request body), 'http.response.body.size' (byte length of response body). The breadcrumb message must be null (not a string). After the breadcrumb is recorded, both the request and response streams must still be fully readable — the instrumentation must NOT consume them.

trigger_keywords: cache breadcrumb message format
hit vs miss vs write breadcrumb text
how cache operations are labeled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cache breadcrumb messages follow these exact patterns: a successful read (cache hit after a prior put) produces 'Read: <key>', a cache miss (get with no prior value) produces 'Missed: <key>', a write/put operation produces its own breadcrumb before any read. The category is always 'cache', level is always 'info', and metadata is an empty array []. When cache breadcrumbs are disabled, no breadcrumb is emitted and breadcrumb_count must be 0 with breadcrumb_exists=false.

trigger_keywords: SQL bindings in breadcrumb
when are query bindings included
record_bindings flag behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When record_queries=true and record_bindings=false, the breadcrumb metadata contains 'connectionName' and 'executionTimeMs' only. When both record_queries=true and record_bindings=true, a 'bindings' array is appended to the metadata. When record_queries=false, no breadcrumb is emitted regardless of record_bindings. The connection name in the test harness is always 'test'.

trigger_keywords: command breadcrumb format
artisan command breadcrumb message
console breadcrumb metadata
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Command/console breadcrumbs use category 'artisan.command', level 'info', message 'Starting Artisan command: <command_name>', and metadata containing a single key 'input' whose value is the arguments joined as a string (e.g. '--foo=bar'). When disabled, breadcrumb_count=0 and breadcrumb_exists=false.

trigger_keywords: filesystem span vs breadcrumb
when are file operations spans vs breadcrumbs
record_spans and record_breadcrumbs flags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Filesystem observability is controlled by two independent boolean flags: record_spans and record_breadcrumbs. When record_spans=true each filesystem operation creates a tracing span. When record_breadcrumbs=true each operation creates a breadcrumb. Both can be active simultaneously or independently. There is always one baseline span (the root transaction span) counted even when no filesystem spans are recorded, so span_count is at minimum 1.

trigger_keywords: filesystem breadcrumb and span metadata structure
what data is attached to file operation spans
disk and driver fields in filesystem observability
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each filesystem span/breadcrumb includes 'disk' (disk name, e.g. 'local') and 'driver' (driver name, e.g. 'local'). For 'put': metadata has 'path', 'options' (empty array), 'disk', 'driver'; description is '<path> (<size> B)'. For 'get': 'path', 'disk', 'driver'; description is the path. For 'delete' with single path: 'path', 'disk', 'driver'; description is the path. For 'delete' with multiple paths: 'paths' (array), 'disk', 'driver'; description is '<count> paths'. For 'files': 'directory' (null if not given), 'recursive' (false by default), 'disk', 'driver'; description is null.

trigger_keywords: database span metadata
server_address and server_port in DB spans
sqlite vs mysql span data
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Database query spans use op 'db.sql.query' with the query text as description. Metadata includes server_address (hostname from connection config) and server_port (integer port). For SQLite/in-memory connections both server_address and server_port must be null. The test harness configures mysql at host-mysql:3306 and mysql_url at host-mysqlurl:3307. Total span_count includes the root transaction span, so it is always at least 2 when a query span is recorded.

trigger_keywords: exception context attachment
what exception context is attached to events
context_mode array vs non_array vs none
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The integration checks whether the thrown exception implements a getContext() method that returns an array. If the exception has no such method (context_mode=none), exception_context is null. If it returns a proper associative array (context_mode=array), that array is serialized as JSON and attached. If getContext() returns a non-array value (context_mode=non_array), the context is treated as absent and exception_context is null.

trigger_keywords: log breadcrumb category naming
how is log level encoded in breadcrumb category
log breadcrumb level field vs category field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Log breadcrumbs use a category of 'log.<level>' (e.g. 'log.debug') and a breadcrumb_level that equals the log level string directly (e.g. 'debug'). The message is the log message string and the metadata is the log context value exactly as passed (which may be an array like ["1"] serialized as a JSON array).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same idea applies here — we want all the usual framework activity to show up automatically
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The framework integration must register listeners for HTTP client response events, cache hit/miss/write/forget events, database query events, log message events, console command start events, and filesystem adapter operations — all wired through the Laravel event system without manual per-call instrumentation.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the other thing (you know, the lighter-weight diagnostic trail)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Breadcrumbs — the Sentry breadcrumb trail recorded via SentrySdk::getCurrentHub()->addBreadcrumb() — as distinct from tracing Spans which are part of the performance monitoring transaction tree.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same pattern as the command breadcrumb feature we scoped out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The enabled/disabled boolean flag pattern used in feature8_console_breadcrumbs.json: a single configuration boolean gates whether the listener records anything at all, and when false the output must show breadcrumb_count=0 and breadcrumb_exists=false.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The apply_to_event hook pattern from feature2_route_transactions.json: the integration stores a transaction name when a route is matched and injects it into Sentry events only when the event does not already carry its own transaction value — preserving any existing event-level transaction.

context_pointers.vague_reference: weird internal strings instead of actual route shapes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Auto-generated Laravel route names matching the pattern 'generated::<alphanumeric>' (e.g. 'generated::KoAePbpBofo01ey4') must be treated as absent/unnamed, causing the transaction name to fall back to the route URL — see feature3_route_name_resolution.json standard router cases.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific technical detail on that one — you'd need to dig into the existing test cases or the codebase to confirm the exact behavior expected there. Check the rcb_tests/public_test_cases folder for the relevant feature file.

feature10_filesystem_breadcrumbs.json.description: Instrumented filesystem operations create breadcrumbs with operation names, descriptions, and disk metadata.

cases.expected_output:
file_content=bar
span_count=1
breadcrumb_count=6
breadcrumb1_exists=true
breadcrumb1_category=file.put
breadcrumb1_level=info
breadcrumb1_message=foo (3 B)
breadcrumb1_metadata={"path":"foo","options":[],"disk":"local","driver":"local"}
breadcrumb2_exists=true
breadcrumb2_category=file.get
breadcrumb2_level=info
breadcrumb2_message=foo
breadcrumb2_metadata={"path":"foo","disk":"local","driver":"local"}
breadcrumb3_exists=true
breadcrumb3_category=file.assertExists
breadcrumb3_level=info
breadcrumb3_message=foo
breadcrumb3_metadata={"path":"foo","disk":"local","driver":"local"}
breadcrumb4_exists=true
breadcrumb4_category=file.delete
breadcrumb4_level=info
breadcrumb4_message=foo
breadcrumb4_metadata={"path":"foo","disk":"local","driver":"local"}
breadcrumb5_exists=true
breadcrumb5_category=file.delete
breadcrumb5_level=info
breadcrumb5_message=2 paths
breadcrumb5_metadata={"paths":["foo","bar"],"disk":"local","driver":"local"}
breadcrumb6_exists=true
breadcrumb6_category=file.files
breadcrumb6_level=info
breadcrumb6_message=null
breadcrumb6_metadata={"directory":null,"recursive":false,"disk":"local","driver":"local"}

feature11_database_spans.json.description: Database query events create spans with SQL descriptions and connection endpoint metadata.

feature12_log_event_mapping.json.description: Log records sent through the monitoring log channel become structured events with context-specific fields.

feature13_exception_context.json.description: Exceptions that expose array context attach that context to events, while missing or non-array context is ignored.

feature1_service_configuration.json.description: Service configuration is reflected in the initialized monitoring client.

cases.expected_output:
monitoring_bound=true
environment=testing
dsn_base=https://sentry.dev
dsn_project_id=123
dsn_public_key=publickey
dsn_secret_key=secretkey
error_types=24575

monitoring_bound=true
environment=override_env
dsn_base=null
dsn_project_id=null
dsn_public_key=null
dsn_secret_key=null
error_types=-1

feature2_route_transactions.json.description: Route events and event application expose the transaction name selected for monitoring events.

feature3_route_name_resolution.json.description: Request route metadata is converted into stable transaction names and source labels.

feature4_http_client_breadcrumbs.json.description: Outbound HTTP responses create breadcrumbs with request and response metadata without consuming streams.

cases.expected_output:
breadcrumb_count=1
breadcrumb_exists=true
breadcrumb_category=http
breadcrumb_level=info
breadcrumb_message=null
breadcrumb_metadata={"url":"https://example.com","http.request.method":"GET","http.response.status_code":200,"http.query":"","http.fragment":"","http.request.body.size":7,"http.response.body.size":8}
request_body_after_breadcrumb=request
response_body_after_breadcrumb=response

feature5_cache_breadcrumbs.json.description: Cache operations emit cache breadcrumbs when enabled and stay silent when disabled.

cases.expected_output:
breadcrumb_count=2
breadcrumb_exists=true
breadcrumb_category=cache
breadcrumb_level=info
breadcrumb_message=Read: foo
breadcrumb_metadata=[]

breadcrumb_count=1
breadcrumb_exists=true
breadcrumb_category=cache
breadcrumb_level=info
breadcrumb_message=Missed: foo
breadcrumb_metadata=[]

feature6_sql_breadcrumbs.json.description: Database query events emit SQL breadcrumbs according to query and binding recording settings.

feature7_log_breadcrumbs.json.description: Application log events emit log breadcrumbs only when log breadcrumbs are enabled.

breadcrumb_count=1
breadcrumb_exists=true
breadcrumb_category=log.debug
breadcrumb_level=debug
breadcrumb_message=test message

feature8_console_breadcrumbs.json.description: Command-start events emit command breadcrumbs with command names and arguments when enabled.

breadcrumb_exists=true

breadcrumb_level=info
breadcrumb_message=Starting Artisan command: test:command
breadcrumb_metadata={"input":"--foo=bar"}
